title: AI over-trust signal
rationale: When a leader is both decision-making in an AI-leaning intuitive mode (IS-leaning or IM-leaning from Assessment 3) AND showing AI-magnification as a cognitive distortion (Assessment 4), they're at meaningful risk of over-relying on AI outputs in consequential calls — the speed of intuitive decision plus the confidence-shape of AI agreement is a known anti-pattern. This combination triggers a short observation email naming the pattern.
emailSubject: Something that showed up across your two results

children.text: Quick observation, not a sales note — feel free to ignore.

Looking at your decision-making style result alongside your distortion read, there's a pattern worth sitting with for thirty seconds. Your decision style leans toward fast, intuitive calls — which is genuinely useful for most decisions in a senior role. And your distortion read flagged an AI-relative pattern: either an over-weighting of AI input on calls where your own judgement should have been louder, or an under-weighting where the AI was actually right.

These two together — fast-decider plus AI-relative distortion — produce a specific failure mode. The AI's confident-sounding output arrives just before the moment you'd normally commit. The combination feels well-supported because the AI agreed. The reality is sometimes that the AI agreed because of how you framed the question.

If this rings true for any decision you've made in the last month, the exercise worth doing is the one I describe in your Cognitive Distortion PDF: reconstruct what you'd have decided without the AI's input. The reconstruction is usually instructive — sometimes the same call, sometimes a meaningfully different one.
No need to reply. Just wanted to flag it.

ctaLabel: If you'd like to talk this through

title: Champion-AI Misalignment + Craft Rising
rationale: When a leader's stakeholder map shows their key champions are on a different AI stance from them (Assessment 5 — champion_ai_misalign tier) OR they have a broader AI-alignment gap (ai_alignment tier), AND their definition of success is shifting toward craft (Assessment 6 — craft_rising), the two are usually related. The misalignment is partly happening because their definition of the work is moving. This combination triggers an observation email naming the two-as-one.

children.text: Brief observation across your two results — feel free to disregard.

Your stakeholder map showed a champion–AI misalignment pattern: the people whose support you most need are on a different AI stance from you. And your success-definition result showed craft rising — your definition of meaningful work is shifting toward doing the work well, on its own terms.

These two read together usually mean one of two things. One: your definition of the work is moving faster than your champions can track, which is producing the misalignment as a side effect. Two: your champions are pulling away from your version of the work because they see it differently, and the craft shift you're feeling is partly your own response to that.

It's worth knowing which one you think is happening. The conversation that follows is different in each case — in the first, you do the work of bringing your champions into your version of the work. In the second, you have an honest conversation about whether you're aligned with the people who matter, and what to do if you're not.
Either of those is worth thirty minutes of a quiet call. No pressure.
